caption.court: HUD, U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development, Office of Fair Housing and Equal Opportunity
caption.caption_note: Retaliation complaint anchored to HACC core housing policies

parties.plaintiff: Aggrieved person / complainant (name to be inserted).
parties.defendant: Housing Authority of Clackamas County (HACC), respondent.

jurisdiction_and_venue: This draft is structured as an administrative housing-discrimination intake for HUD and should be tailored to the final statutory basis asserted.
HUD jurisdiction and timeliness should be confirmed against the final incident dates, protected-basis theory, and requested relief.
The dispute appears to arise from housing-program administration, adverse action, and procedural protections connected to HACC operations.
factual_allegations: The complaint centers on a retaliation and grievance-related housing complaint involving HACC notice and review protections.
The complainant contends that the grievance or hearing process was not handled in the manner described by HACC policy.
The complainant contends that appeal rights and due-process protections were not clearly honored.
The core documentary support for these allegations appears in Exhibit A (ADMISSIONS AND CONTINUED OCCUPANCY POLICY) and Exhibit B (ADMINISTRATIVE PLAN).
claims_theory: Fair Housing Act anti-retaliation provisions, 24 C.F.R. Part 100 may be implicated if HACC used grievance, review, or adverse-action procedures to respond to protected complaints or protected activity.

The available policy language suggests the complainant should have received an informal review or hearing, written notice, and a review decision, but the intake narrative describes those protections as missing or unclear.
The complainant also describes a retaliation theory based on the timing of the adverse treatment after protected complaints or grievance activity.

The policy theory is grounded in HACC language stating that If HACC denies a request for an accommodation because it is not reasonable (it would impose an undue financial and administrative burden or fundamentally alter the nature of HACC’s operations), HACC will notify the family in writing of its decision. The notice will inform the family of the right to appeal HACC’s decision through an informal hearing (if ap... That documentary support is reflected in Exhibit A (ADMISSIONS AND CONTINUED OCCUPANCY POLICY) and Exhibit B (ADMINISTRATIVE PLAN).
policy_basis: ADMISSIONS AND CONTINUED OCCUPANCY POLICY supports grievance hearing, appeal rights: [hearing, adverse_action] HACC policy defines a grievance as a tenant dispute concerning HACC action or inaction. Full passage: I. Definitions applicable to the grievance procedure [24 CFR 966.53] A. Grievance: Any dispute a tenant may have with respect to HACC action or failure to act in accordance with the individual tenant’s lease or HACC regulations that adversely affects the individual tenant’s rights, duties, welfare, or status. B. Complainant: Any tenant (as defined below) whose grievance is presented to HACC or at the project management office in accordance with the requirements presented in this procedure. C. Elements of due process: An eviction action or a termination of tenancy in a state or local court in which the following procedural safeguards are required: i.

ADMINISTRATIVE PLAN supports grievance hearing, appeal rights: [notice, hearing, adverse_action] HACC policy describes scheduling and procedures for informal review. Full passage: Notice to the Applicant [24 CFR 982.554(a)] HACC must give an applicant prompt notice of a decision denying assistance. The notice must contain a brief statement of the reasons for HACC decision and must also state that the applicant may request an informal review of the decision. The notice must describe how to obtain the informal review. Scheduling an Informal Review HACC Policy A request for an informal review must be made in writing and delivered to HACC either in person or by first class mail, by the close of the business day, no later than 10 business days from the date of HACC’s denial of assistance.

causes_of_action.title: Administrative Fair Housing Process Failure
causes_of_action.theory: The draft facts suggest denial or termination activity without the clear notice, review, or hearing process described by HACC policy.
support: Fair Housing Act anti-retaliation provisions, 24 C.F.R. Part 100 may be implicated if HACC used grievance, review, or adverse-action procedures to respond to protected complaints or protected activity.

The available policy language suggests the complainant should have received an informal review or hearing, written notice, and a review decision, but the intake narrative describes those protections as missing or unclear.
Documentary support: Exhibit B (ADMINISTRATIVE PLAN). Rationale: selected for stronger notice and process language.
causes_of_action.selection_rationale: selected for stronger notice and process language

causes_of_action.title: Retaliation for Protected Fair Housing Activity
causes_of_action.theory: The complainant narrative suggests adverse treatment after raising concerns or invoking grievance protections.
support: Fair Housing Act anti-retaliation provisions, 24 C.F.R. Part 100 may be implicated if HACC used grievance, review, or adverse-action procedures to respond to protected complaints or protected activity.
The complainant also describes a retaliation theory based on the timing of the adverse treatment after protected complaints or grievance activity.
Documentary support: Exhibit B (ADMINISTRATIVE PLAN). Rationale: selected for the strongest overlap with grievance activity and adverse-process protections tied to the retaliation theory.

The notice will inform the family of the right to appeal HACC’s decision through an informal hearing (if ap...
The intake record suggests the grievance or hearing process was not handled in the manner described by HACC policy.

Case-specific facts still need confirmation, including the date and nature of the adverse action, whether written notice, an informal review, a grievance hearing, or an appeal was requested or denied, who at HACC made or communicated the decision, the resulting housing harm and requested remedy.
requested_relief: Administrative investigation of the challenged housing practices and adverse-action process.
Corrective action requiring clear notice, fair review, and non-retaliation safeguards.
Appropriate administrative remedies, damages, and other relief authorized by fair housing law.
Any additional relief HUD is authorized to obtain or recommend.
